did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Single Shot Multibox Detector (SSD), a deep convolutional neural network (CNN) model, to classify and detect skin lesions (mycosis fungoides, psoriasis, atopic dermatitis, and normal skin) from 292 test images, achieving a diagnosis accuracy of 93%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the construction and validation of a Single Shot Multibox Detector (SSD) model for classifying skin lesions. It details specific methods including sample preparation (948 training images, 292 test images), ethical approval, and the use of a CNN architecture. The results section reports new diagnostic accuracy metrics (93% overall accuracy, specific sensitivities and precisions for MF, PSO, AD, and normal skin) derived from the authors' own data collection and model testing, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Identification of Skin Lesions by Using Single-Step Multiframe Detector
brief_summary: This study developed a Single Shot Multibox Detector (SSD) deep convolutional neural network to classify skin lesions into mycosis fungoides (MF), psoriasis (PSO), atopic dermatitis (AD), and normal skin. The model was trained on 948 images and evaluated on 292 test images sourced from Chung Shan Medical University Hospital, with ground truth verified by pathological tissue slices and optical coherence tomography. The SSD achieved an overall diagnosis accuracy of 93%, processing the test set in 10 seconds. Sensitivity and precision varied by class, with MF showing high sensitivity (94%) and PSO showing high precision (96%), while AD diagnosis was relatively poorer due to smaller lesion areas and less obvious features. The results suggest that the proposed SSD can assist clinicians in quickly identifying these skin diseases, although performance for AD could be improved with more diverse image data.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four primary classes studied. Included 215 training images and 50 test images. The paper notes MF is a rare disease and a type of cancer, distinguished from common skin cancers like melanoma, BCC, and SCC which are mentioned only for context.
One of the four primary classes studied. Included 421 training images and 139 test images. Described as having obvious symptoms like desquamation and patches.

One of the four primary classes studied. Included 139 training images and 40 test images. Performance was poorer due to smaller lesion areas and less obvious features, often located on elbows or arms.
One of the four primary classes studied (control/comparator). Included 173 training images and 63 test images. Used as a baseline for classification against pathological lesions.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (Mycosis fungoides, psoriasis, atopic dermatitis, normal skin) with bounding boxes.
all_task_types.details: The paper uses a Single Shot Multibox Detector (SSD) to detect and classify skin lesions. The model outputs disease names and rectangular bounding boxes surrounding the lesion areas. It performs multiclass classification (4 classes: MF, PSO, AD, Normal) within a detection framework, reporting sensitivity, precision, and accuracy for each class.

datasets_used.name: Chung Shan Medical University Hospital (CSMUH) Dermatology Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private clinical dataset of skin lesion images collected from the Department of Dermatology at Chung Shan Medical University Hospital. Images were captured using a digital camera (E-M10 Mark III/Olympus) by doctors on patient lesion locations. Ground truth was verified by pathological tissue slices and optical coherence tomography (OCT).

use.training: Used to train the Single Shot Multibox Detector (SSD) model. Data augmentation was applied (details in Supplementary Information S2).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the diagnostic accuracy, sensitivity, precision, and F1-score of the trained SSD model.
use.other: Ground truth verification was performed using pathological tissue slices and OCT analysis.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate and display diagnostic performance metrics including sensitivity, precision, F1-score, and accuracy for each class (PSO, AD, MF, Normal) and overall.
To calculate the arithmetic mean of statistical indicator values across all categories.
To establish a global confusion matrix for each model example in the dataset without category distinction and calculate corresponding indicators.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics including accuracy, sensitivity, precision, and F1-score derived from a confusion matrix.

Chung Shan Medical University Hospital (.notes: The SSD model diagnosed 273 out of 292 test images correctly. The diagnosis accuracy for AD was relatively poor (sensitivity 80%) due to smaller lesion areas, less obvious features, and images often being taken of arms/elbows at low magnification. MF showed high sensitivity (94%) likely due to large lesion areas from cancer cell spread. PSO and MF had the best F1-scores (96%). The paper notes a discrepancy in the text stating 90% accuracy in one sentence but 93% in the abstract and Table 2 caption; Table 2 and the abstract confirm 93% (273/292).

architecture_used.pretraining: VGG16 backbone (implied by reference to Conv4_3 layer after VGG16 model)

architecture_details.normalization: L2 Normalization applied to Conv4_3 layer

training_details.data_augmentation: Referenced in Supplementary Information S2 (details not provided in text)
training_details.implementation: SSD-HS, Hitspectra Intelligent Technology Co., Ltd., Taiwan

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Bounding box coordinates and class probabilities

preprocessing_techniques: L2 Normalization applied to the Conv4_3 layer features
Non-maximum suppression for eliminating redundant bounding boxes

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper states that data augmentation was applied to the training images, but the specific techniques, probabilities, or settings are not provided in the main text; they are referenced as being available in Supplementary Information S2.

conclusions: The Single Shot Multibox Detector (SSD) model achieved an overall diagnosis accuracy of 93% (273 out of 292 images correctly detected) in classifying mycosis fungoides (MF), psoriasis (PSO), atopic dermatitis (AD), and normal skin.
The SSD model processed the test set of 292 images in 10 seconds, demonstrating rapid diagnostic capability.
The model showed high sensitivity for MF (94%) and PSO (96%), attributed to large lesion areas and obvious symptoms like desquamation and patches.
The diagnosis of AD was relatively poor (sensitivity 80%) due to smaller lesion areas, less obvious features, and images often being taken of arms or elbows at low magnification.
The proposed SSD technology can assist clinicians in quickly identifying and diagnosing skin lesions, promoting early detection.
Performance for AD and other classes could be improved in the future by establishing a larger and more diverse image database with increased sample sizes for each category.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single-center dataset from Chung Shan Medical University Hospital, raising concerns about external validation and generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

Atopic dermatitis (AD) diagnosis performance was notably weaker (sensitivity 80%) compared to other classes, attributed to smaller lesion areas, less obvious features, and images often being taken of arms/elbows at low magnification.

There is a discrepancy in the reported overall accuracy: the text states 90% (273/292 is actually 93.5%, but the text explicitly says '90%' in one sentence while Table 2 caption and abstract say '93%'), while Table 2 and the abstract report 93% (273/292).

The dataset is imbalanced, with Psoriasis (PSO) having the most samples (421 training, 139 test) and Atopic Dermatitis (AD) having the fewest (139 training, 40 test), which may bias the model towards PSO.
Data augmentation details are not provided in the main text but are referenced in Supplementary Information S2, limiting reproducibility from the main article alone.

The ground truth verification relied on pathological tissue slices and optical coherence tomography (OCT), which provides robust clinical validation but limits the dataset to cases where these invasive or specialized tests were performed.

The model was implemented using a commercial software package (SSD-HS, Hitspectra Intelligent Technology Co., Ltd.), and the specific hyperparameters or code implementation are not fully disclosed, relying on Supplementary Information S3 for architecture details.
The study does not report statistical significance testing (p-values) for the performance metrics, relying solely on descriptive accuracy, sensitivity, and precision.
